options.text: Polio is caused by a parasite; PPS is prevented by surgery.
options.explanation: Polio is not caused by a parasite, and PPS is not prevented by surgery.

options.text: Polio only affects the lungs; PPS is an autoimmune disease.
options.explanation: Polio primarily affects the nervous system, not only the lungs, and PPS is not an autoimmune disease.

question: Which statement best describes Q fever?

options.text: A zoonotic infection caused by Coxiella burnetii, usually acquired by inhalation from infected animals
options.explanation: Q fever is caused by Coxiella burnetii and is typically acquired by inhaling contaminated aerosols from infected animals.

options.text: A viral illness spread mainly by human-to-human respiratory droplets
options.explanation: Q fever is not a viral disease and human-to-human spread is rare.

options.text: A parasitic disease transmitted primarily by mosquito bites
options.explanation: Q fever is not parasitic and is not primarily transmitted by mosquitoes.

options.text: A fungal infection acquired from contaminated soil only
options.explanation: Q fever is bacterial, not fungal, and transmission is not limited to contaminated soil.

question: Which description best defines chronic active Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection?

options.text: A rare EBV complication causing persistent lymphoproliferation with fever and organ involvement
options.explanation: Chronic active EBV infection is a rare life-threatening EBV-associated lymphoproliferative disorder with persistent symptoms and organ involvement.

options.text: A common childhood EBV infection that is usually asymptomatic
options.explanation: Most childhood EBV infections are asymptomatic and do not represent chronic active disease.

options.text: Acute EBV mononucleosis with sore throat and self-limited fatigue
options.explanation: Acute infectious mononucleosis is a short-term EBV illness, not chronic active infection.

options.text: A bacterial infection causing lymph node enlargement and thrombocytosis
options.explanation: CAEBV is viral, not bacterial, and it is associated with thrombocytopenia rather than thrombocytosis.

question: What is malaria?

options.text: A parasitic disease transmitted by infected mosquitoes
options.explanation: Malaria is caused by a parasite and transmitted through bites from infected mosquitoes.

options.text: A viral illness spread by contaminated water
options.explanation: Malaria is not spread by contaminated water and is not caused by a virus.

options.text: A bacterial infection causing skin ulcers
options.explanation: Malaria does not cause skin ulcers and is not a bacterial infection.

options.text: An autoimmune disorder affecting red blood cells
options.explanation: Malaria is not an autoimmune disease, though it can affect red blood cells.

question: What best describes respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection?

options.text: A viral illness causing mild colds in adults but severe disease in infants
options.explanation: RSV usually causes mild cold-like illness in adults but can cause severe respiratory disease in young infants.

options.text: A bacterial pneumonia treated with antibiotics
options.explanation: RSV is caused by a virus, not bacteria, so antibiotics are not specific treatment.

options.text: A fungal infection causing chronic cough in children
options.explanation: RSV is not a fungal infection and does not typically cause chronic fungal lung disease.

options.text: A parasitic disease spread by mosquitoes
options.explanation: RSV is spread by respiratory contact and contaminated surfaces, not by mosquitoes.

question: What is the most common cause of necrotizing fasciitis?

options.text: Klebsiella species
options.explanation: Klebsiella can cause necrotizing fasciitis, but it is not the most common cause.

options.text: Group A Streptococcus
options.explanation: Group A Streptococcus is the most common cause of necrotizing fasciitis.

options.text: Escherichia coli
options.explanation: Escherichia coli may be involved, but it is less common than group A Streptococcus.

options.text: Clostridium species
options.explanation: Clostridium can cause severe soft tissue infection, but it is not the leading cause of necrotizing fasciitis.

question: Which statement best describes Legionnaires' disease?

options.text: A bacterial pneumonia spread by inhaling contaminated water mist
options.explanation: Legionnaires' disease is a bacterial pneumonia acquired by inhaling contaminated water aerosols.

options.text: A viral pneumonia spread by direct person-to-person contact
options.explanation: Legionnaires' disease does not spread person to person and is not caused by a virus.

options.text: A fungal lung infection acquired from soil exposure
options.explanation: It is not a fungal infection and is not typically acquired from soil exposure.

options.text: An autoimmune inflammation of the lungs triggered by allergens
options.explanation: It is an infectious pneumonia, not an autoimmune or allergic lung disease.

question: What is the most common cause of Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome?

options.text: Meningococcal infection
options.explanation: Meningococcal disease causes most cases of Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.

options.text: Viral hepatitis
options.explanation: Viral hepatitis is not a typical cause of this adrenal hemorrhagic syndrome.

options.text: Autoimmune thyroid disease
options.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid disease does not cause Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.

options.text: Iron deficiency anemia
options.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is unrelated to adrenal hemorrhage and septic shock.

question: Which set of symptoms best describes Chapare hemorrhagic fever (CHHF)?

options.text: Fever, headache, muscle pain, vomiting, then hemorrhagic signs
options.explanation: The reported CHHF case began with fever, headache, myalgia/arthralgia, and vomiting, then progressed to hemorrhagic signs.

options.text: Cough, wheezing, chest pain, and hemoptysis
options.explanation: Respiratory symptoms like cough and wheezing are not the typical described presentation of CHHF.

options.text: Diarrhea, jaundice, rash, and conjunctivitis
options.explanation: These findings suggest other infections and are not the classic symptoms reported for CHHF.

options.text: Abdominal pain, dysuria, edema, and hypertension
options.explanation: These symptoms are not characteristic of the described CHHF clinical course.

question: Which description best defines herpes zoster oticus?

options.text: Varicella-zoster reactivation affecting facial nerves and the ear
options.explanation: Herpes zoster oticus is shingles from varicella-zoster virus reactivation involving facial nerves and the ear.

options.text: Primary bacterial infection of the external ear canal
options.explanation: This describes otitis externa, not a viral reactivation syndrome.

options.text: Autoimmune demyelination causing bilateral hearing loss
options.explanation: Demyelination is not the cause of herpes zoster oticus.

options.text: Middle ear effusion causing conductive hearing loss
options.explanation: Middle ear effusion causes conductive loss but does not cause the rash and facial palsy of herpes zoster oticus.

question: Which statement best describes foodborne illness?

options.text: Illness caused by contaminated food or drink, often from bacteria, viruses, or parasites
options.explanation: Foodborne illness results from consuming contaminated food or drink, commonly due to bacteria, viruses, or parasites.

options.text: Illness caused only by food allergies after eating peanuts or shellfish
options.explanation: Food allergies can cause reactions, but they are not the definition of foodborne illness.

options.text: Illness caused by vitamin deficiency from an unbalanced diet
options.explanation: Vitamin deficiency is a nutritional disorder, not an infection from contaminated food.

options.text: Illness caused only by toxins produced in spoiled canned foods
options.explanation: Spoiled canned foods can cause botulism, but foodborne illness includes many other causes as well.

question: What are tinea infections?

options.text: Fungal skin infections such as ringworm, athlete's foot, and jock itch
options.explanation: Tinea refers to a group of fungal infections including ringworm, athlete's foot, and jock itch.

options.text: Bacterial infections causing pus-filled skin lesions
options.explanation: Bacterial infections do not define tinea and typically cause different lesion types.

options.text: Viral infections causing vesicular rashes
options.explanation: Viral infections are not the cause of tinea, which is fungal.

options.text: Autoimmune disorders affecting the skin
options.explanation: Autoimmune disorders are not infectious and do not describe tinea infections.

question: What is fifth disease caused by?

options.text: Parvovirus B19 infection
options.explanation: Fifth disease is caused by human parvovirus B19.

options.text: Varicella-zoster virus infection
options.explanation: Varicella-zoster virus causes chickenpox and shingles, not fifth disease.

options.text: Measles virus infection
options.explanation: Measles virus causes measles, which has a different rash and symptoms.

options.text: Epstein-Barr virus infection
options.explanation: Epstein-Barr virus causes infectious mononucleosis, not fifth disease.

question: Which measure best helps prevent the spread of body lice?

options.text: Bathe regularly and change into clean, laundered clothes weekly
options.explanation: Regular bathing and changing into clean, laundered clothes reduces body lice transmission.

options.text: Use antibiotics on all household contacts
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent or control lice infestation.

options.text: Apply topical antifungal cream daily
options.explanation: Antifungal creams treat fungal infections, not lice.

options.text: Avoid all outdoor activities for two weeks
options.explanation: Avoiding outdoor activities does not address lice spread from clothing or bedding.

question: Which statement best describes the common cold?

options.text: A viral upper respiratory infection causing sneezing, sore throat, and cough
options.explanation: The common cold is a viral upper respiratory infection with sneezing, sore throat, stuffy nose, and cough.

options.text: A bacterial lung infection causing high fever and productive cough
options.explanation: Bacterial lung infections typically cause pneumonia, not the usual mild cold symptoms.

options.text: An allergic reaction causing wheezing and hives after exposure to pollen
options.explanation: Allergies can cause nasal symptoms, but they are not an infectious cold illness.

options.text: A gastrointestinal infection causing vomiting and diarrhea
options.explanation: Gastrointestinal infections affect the stomach and intestines, not the upper respiratory tract.

question: Which statement best describes pneumococcal infections?

options.text: Infections caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, ranging from ear infections to meningitis
options.explanation: Pneumococcal infections are caused by pneumococci (Streptococcus pneumoniae) and can cause ear infections, sinusitis, pneumonia, sepsis, and meningitis.

options.text: Viral infections primarily affecting the gastrointestinal tract
options.explanation: Pneumococcal infections are bacterial, not viral, and commonly affect the respiratory tract and invasive sites.

options.text: Fungal infections spread only by contaminated food
options.explanation: They are not fungal and are spread by respiratory contact or carriage, not contaminated food.

options.text: Parasitic infections limited to the skin
options.explanation: They are not parasitic and do not only involve the skin.

question: Which measure best helps prevent trichinellosis from undercooked pork?

options.text: Freeze pork under 6 inches thick for 20 days at 5°F (-15°C)
options.explanation: Freezing pork at the recommended temperature and duration can kill Trichinella larvae in pork products.

options.text: Curing or smoking meat alone reliably kills Trichinella
options.explanation: Curing or smoking alone does not consistently kill infective worms.

options.text: Microwaving meat briefly always eliminates infective worms
options.explanation: Microwaving alone is not a reliable method to eliminate Trichinella.

options.text: Freezing wild game meat always kills all Trichinella species
options.explanation: Wild game may contain freeze-resistant species, so freezing does not always kill all worms.

question: What is toxoplasmosis?

options.text: A parasitic infection caused by Toxoplasma gondii
options.explanation: Toxoplasmosis is caused by the parasite Toxoplasma gondii.

options.text: A bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica
options.explanation: Salmonella causes foodborne bacterial illness, not toxoplasmosis.

options.text: A viral infection caused by herpes simplex virus
options.explanation: Herpes simplex virus causes viral infections, not a parasitic disease.

options.text: A fungal infection caused by Candida albicans
options.explanation: Candida albicans causes fungal infections, not toxoplasmosis.

question: Which statement best describes trichomoniasis?

options.text: A parasitic sexually transmitted infection often causing vaginal discharge and dysuria
options.explanation: Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted parasitic infection that can cause discharge, itching, and painful urination.

options.text: A viral infection causing genital ulcers and systemic rash
options.explanation: Genital ulcers and systemic rash are more typical of viral infections like herpes or syphilis-related disease.

options.text: A bacterial infection spread by contaminated food and water
options.explanation: Trichomoniasis is not spread by food or water; it is transmitted through sexual intercourse.

options.text: A fungal infection causing thick white vaginal discharge
options.explanation: Thick white discharge is more characteristic of candidiasis, not trichomoniasis.

question: Which statement about hepatitis A is most accurate?

options.text: It is a viral liver infection spread by contact with infected stool.
options.explanation: Hepatitis A is a virus that causes liver inflammation and is spread through contact with infected stool.

options.text: It is a bacterial infection spread mainly by respiratory droplets.
options.explanation: Hepatitis A is not bacterial and is not primarily spread by respiratory droplets.

options.text: It always causes severe jaundice in all infected children.
options.explanation: Most children, especially under age 6, may have no symptoms or only mild illness.

options.text: It requires long-term antiviral treatment to resolve.
options.explanation: Hepatitis A usually resolves on its own in a few weeks without specific treatment.

question: Which statement best describes Stenotrophomonas maltophilia infection?

options.text: A community-acquired viral illness causing pneumonia in healthy children
options.explanation: It is not a viral illness and does not typically affect healthy children in the community.

options.text: A healthcare-associated infection in hospitalized, immunocompromised patients
options.explanation: S. maltophilia is an opportunistic healthcare-associated bacterial infection, especially in hospitalized or immunocompromised patients.

options.text: A fungal infection primarily affecting the skin after swimming
options.explanation: It is a bacterial, not fungal, infection and is not primarily a skin infection from swimming.

options.text: A parasitic infection transmitted by contaminated food
options.explanation: It is not a parasitic disease and is not transmitted through contaminated food.

question: Which test best diagnoses loiasis by detecting microfilariae in peripheral blood?

options.text: Blood smear collected between 10 AM and 2 PM
options.explanation: Loa loa microfilariae show daytime periodicity, so blood drawn between 10 AM and 2 PM is most diagnostic.

options.text: Stool ova and parasite examination
options.explanation: Loiasis is not diagnosed by stool examination because the parasite is blood-borne, not intestinal.

options.text: Nighttime thick blood smear
options.explanation: Nighttime smears miss Loa loa because microfilariae are typically present in daytime blood samples.

options.text: Urine antigen test
options.explanation: Urine antigen testing is not a standard diagnostic method for loiasis.

question: What are tick bites?

options.text: Bites from small bloodsucking parasites that can transmit disease
options.explanation: Ticks are small bloodsucking parasites that can transmit diseases to humans and animals.

options.text: Bites from mosquitoes that spread viral infections
options.explanation: Mosquitoes are different vectors and are not ticks.

options.text: Stings from insects that inject venom into skin
options.explanation: Stings with venom are caused by insects like bees, not ticks.

options.text: Bites from spiders that cause local skin necrosis
options.explanation: Spider bites are not tick bites and have different mechanisms and effects.

question: Which statement best describes Lyme disease?

options.text: A tickborne bacterial infection caused by Borrelia burgdorferi
options.explanation: Lyme disease is caused by Borrelia burgdorferi and transmitted by infected blacklegged ticks.

options.text: A viral illness spread by mosquitoes causing rash and fever
options.explanation: Lyme disease is not a mosquito-borne viral infection.

options.text: An autoimmune disease primarily affecting the kidneys
options.explanation: Lyme disease is not primarily an autoimmune kidney disorder.

options.text: A fungal infection transmitted through contaminated food
options.explanation: Lyme disease is not a fungal infection and is not spread through food.

question: How can hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS) be prevented?

options.text: Eliminate rodent contact and reduce infestation in homes and campsites
options.explanation: Preventing HPS focuses on minimizing contact with rodents and their droppings by sealing entry points, trapping rodents, and removing food sources.

options.text: Take prophylactic antibiotics after rodent exposure
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent a viral infection like hantavirus pulmonary syndrome.

options.text: Receive a routine antiviral vaccine annually
options.explanation: There is no routine vaccine available for preventing HPS.

options.text: Avoid all outdoor activity during summer months
options.explanation: HPS prevention is not based on avoiding summer outdoor activity, but on rodent exposure reduction.

question: What are yeast infections caused by?

options.text: Candida, a fungus that can overgrow and infect the body
options.explanation: Yeast infections are caused by Candida, a fungus that normally lives in and on the body but can overgrow.

options.text: Bacteria that invade the skin and mucous membranes
options.explanation: Bacteria do not cause yeast infections, which are fungal infections.

options.text: Viruses that cause white patches and discharge
options.explanation: Viruses do not produce Candida-related thrush or vaginal yeast infections.

options.text: Parasites that live only in the bloodstream
options.explanation: Parasites are not the cause of yeast infections and do not explain Candida overgrowth.

question: Which set of findings is most consistent with Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever?

options.text: Sudden fever, headache, myalgia, vomiting, and later bleeding
options.explanation: CCHF typically begins abruptly with fever, headache, myalgias, gastrointestinal symptoms, and progresses to hemorrhagic manifestations.

options.text: Gradual cough, wheezing, and pleuritic chest pain
options.explanation: These symptoms suggest a respiratory illness, not the characteristic febrile hemorrhagic syndrome of CCHF.

options.text: Painless jaundice, pruritus, and clay-colored stools
options.explanation: This pattern is more consistent with cholestatic liver disease than viral hemorrhagic fever.

options.text: Neck stiffness, photophobia, and altered consciousness
options.explanation: These findings are more typical of meningitis or encephalitis, not CCHF.

question: What is whooping cough?

options.text: A bacterial infection causing severe coughing fits and a whooping sound
options.explanation: Whooping cough is pertussis, a bacterial disease marked by severe coughing spells and a whooping inspiration.

options.text: A viral infection causing a barking cough and hoarseness
options.explanation: A viral croup-like illness causes barking cough and hoarseness, not the classic whoop.

options.text: An allergic reaction causing wheezing and hives
options.explanation: Allergic reactions can cause wheezing, but they do not cause infectious coughing spells with whooping.

options.text: A fungal lung infection causing chronic fever and weight loss
options.explanation: Fungal lung infections can cause chronic respiratory symptoms, but not the characteristic pertussis cough.

question: Which statement best describes schistosomiasis?

options.text: A parasitic worm infection acquired through contaminated water
options.explanation: Schistosomiasis is caused by parasitic worms acquired when cercariae in contaminated water penetrate the skin.

options.text: A bacterial infection spread by mosquito bites
options.explanation: Mosquito bites transmit malaria and other diseases, not schistosomiasis.

options.text: A viral illness transmitted by respiratory droplets
options.explanation: Schistosomiasis is not a viral respiratory infection.

options.text: A fungal infection from soil exposure
options.explanation: Soil exposure causes some fungal infections, but not schistosomiasis.

question: What best describes coronavirus infections?

options.text: Common viruses causing mostly mild upper respiratory illness
options.explanation: Coronaviruses are common viruses that usually cause mild to moderate upper-respiratory illness.

options.text: Bacterial infections requiring antibiotics for cure
options.explanation: Coronaviruses are viruses, not bacteria, and antibiotics do not treat them.

options.text: Parasitic infections spread only by contaminated food
options.explanation: They are not parasites and are not spread only through contaminated food.

options.text: Fungal infections prevented by vaccination
options.explanation: They are not fungal infections, and there is no vaccine to prevent them.

question: What is mycetoma?

options.text: A chronic infection caused by fungi or actinomycetes with swelling and draining sinus tracts
options.explanation: Mycetoma is a chronic fungal or actinomycete infection that causes swelling, nodules, and draining sinus tracts with grains.

options.text: An acute viral infection causing painful vesicular skin lesions
options.explanation: Mycetoma is not an acute viral illness and does not typically cause vesicular skin lesions.

options.text: A hereditary disorder causing painless joint deformities
options.explanation: Mycetoma is an acquired infection, not a hereditary condition causing joint deformities.

options.text: A parasitic infection limited to the gastrointestinal tract
options.explanation: Mycetoma is a subcutaneous infection, not a gastrointestinal parasitic disease.

question: What is the most common cause of urinary tract infection in adults?

options.text: Escherichia coli from bowel flora
options.explanation: E. coli from the bowel causes the vast majority of adult UTIs.

options.text: Chlamydia trachomatis infection
options.explanation: Chlamydia usually infects the urethra and reproductive tract, not the bladder.

options.text: Mycoplasma genitalium infection
options.explanation: Mycoplasma can cause urethritis, but it is not the main cause of bladder infection.

options.text: Candida albicans overgrowth
options.explanation: Candida can cause urinary infection in some settings, but it is not the usual cause in adults.

question: Which statement best describes Helicobacter pylori infection?

options.text: A stomach bacterial infection linked to ulcers and gastric cancer
options.explanation: H. pylori is a stomach bacterium associated with peptic ulcers and stomach cancer.

options.text: A viral infection causing acute gastroenteritis
options.explanation: H. pylori is not a virus and does not cause acute viral gastroenteritis.

options.text: A fungal infection of the small intestine
options.explanation: H. pylori is not a fungus and primarily infects the stomach, not the small intestine.

options.text: A parasitic infection causing hepatitis
options.explanation: H. pylori is not a parasite and does not cause hepatitis.

question: Which treatment is first-line for invasive aspergillosis?

options.text: Oral prednisone
options.explanation: Prednisone is used for allergic aspergillosis, not invasive fungal infection.

options.text: Intravenous voriconazole
options.explanation: Voriconazole is the current first-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis and is usually given intravenously.

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate
options.explanation: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is an antibacterial drug and does not treat aspergillosis.

options.text: Acyclovir
options.explanation: Acyclovir is an antiviral medication and has no role in treating aspergillosis.

question: Which statement best describes rabies?

options.text: A viral disease spread by bites from infected animals
options.explanation: Rabies is a deadly viral disease transmitted to humans through bites from infected animals.

options.text: A bacterial infection spread by contaminated water
options.explanation: Rabies is not caused by bacteria or spread through contaminated water.

options.text: A parasitic disease transmitted by mosquito bites
options.explanation: Rabies is not a parasitic disease and is not transmitted by mosquitoes.

options.text: A fungal illness acquired from soil exposure
options.explanation: Rabies is not a fungal infection and is not acquired from soil exposure.

question: What is chancroid?

options.text: A sexually transmitted infection caused by Haemophilus ducreyi
options.explanation: Chancroid is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection caused by Haemophilus ducreyi.

options.text: A viral infection causing painless genital ulcers
options.explanation: Painless genital ulcers are more typical of syphilis, not chancroid.

options.text: A fungal infection causing vulvar itching
options.explanation: Fungal infections usually cause itching and discharge, not painful genital ulcers.

options.text: An autoimmune disease causing oral ulcers
options.explanation: Autoimmune disease does not cause chancroid's infectious painful ulcer and buboes.

question: Which statement best describes fungal infections?

options.text: Infections caused by primitive organisms like molds and yeasts
options.explanation: Fungal infections are caused by fungi, primitive organisms such as molds and yeasts.

options.text: Infections caused only by bacteria in the bloodstream
options.explanation: Bacterial bloodstream infections are not fungal infections.

options.text: Infections caused only by viruses affecting the skin
options.explanation: Viruses do not cause fungal infections.

options.text: Infections caused by parasites transmitted by mosquitoes
options.explanation: Mosquito-borne parasites cause parasitic infections, not fungal infections.

question: What are Haemophilus infections?

options.text: Bacterial infections causing respiratory, bone, joint, and nervous system disease
options.explanation: Haemophilus are bacteria that can cause illnesses involving the breathing system, bones and joints, and the nervous system.

options.text: Viral infections limited to the skin and mucous membranes
options.explanation: Haemophilus are bacteria, not viruses, and they can cause invasive systemic disease.

options.text: Fungal infections primarily affecting the gastrointestinal tract
options.explanation: Haemophilus are not fungi and do not primarily cause gastrointestinal infections.

options.text: Parasitic infections causing only urinary symptoms
options.explanation: Haemophilus are not parasites and cause more than urinary symptoms.

question: Which test is used to diagnose Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever during the acute phase?

options.text: Antigen-capture ELISA and RT-PCR on blood
options.explanation: Acute CCHF is diagnosed by detecting viral antigen or RNA with antigen-capture ELISA and RT-PCR.

options.text: Widal test and stool culture
options.explanation: Widal test and stool culture diagnose enteric infections, not viral hemorrhagic fever.

options.text: Mantoux test and chest X-ray
options.explanation: Mantoux test and chest X-ray are used for tuberculosis evaluation, not CCHF diagnosis.

options.text: Serum amylase and lipase
options.explanation: Serum amylase and lipase assess pancreatitis, not Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever.

question: Which statement best describes infectious diseases?

options.text: Diseases caused by germs that can spread between people, animals, or the environment
options.explanation: Infectious diseases are caused by germs that can be transmitted through contact, food, water, air, bites, or sexual exposure.

options.text: Noninfectious disorders caused only by inherited gene mutations
options.explanation: Inherited gene mutations cause genetic disorders, not infectious diseases.

options.text: Conditions caused exclusively by vitamin deficiencies
options.explanation: Vitamin deficiencies cause nutritional diseases, not infections.

options.text: Autoimmune diseases triggered only by the body's own tissues
options.explanation: Autoimmune diseases result from immune dysfunction, not from infectious germs.

question: What is impetigo?

options.text: A bacterial skin infection, usually staph or strep
options.explanation: Impetigo is a contagious bacterial skin infection most often caused by staphylococci or streptococci.

options.text: A viral rash caused by herpes simplex
options.explanation: Herpes simplex causes viral vesicular lesions, not the typical crusted bacterial sores of impetigo.

options.text: A fungal infection of hair follicles
options.explanation: Fungal folliculitis affects hair follicles and is not the usual cause of impetigo.

options.text: An autoimmune blistering skin disease
options.explanation: Autoimmune blistering diseases are immune-mediated, whereas impetigo is caused by bacteria.

question: Who is at risk for Marburg hemorrhagic fever?

options.text: People with unprotected bat exposure or contact with infected body fluids
options.explanation: Marburg virus can spread from infected bats and then person-to-person through direct contact with body fluids or contaminated materials.

options.text: People who eat undercooked pork from endemic areas
options.explanation: Undercooked pork is associated with other infections, not Marburg hemorrhagic fever.

options.text: People exposed only to mosquito bites
options.explanation: Mosquito bites are not a known route of Marburg virus transmission.

options.text: People with seasonal pollen allergies
options.explanation: Pollen allergies do not cause viral hemorrhagic fever.

question: What is the mainstay of treatment for botulism?

options.text: Supportive hospital care with antitoxin administration
options.explanation: Botulism is treated with supportive hospital care and antitoxin to block circulating toxin.

options.text: High-dose corticosteroids and immunosuppressants
options.explanation: Corticosteroids and immunosuppressants do not treat botulinum toxin effects.

options.text: Immediate anticoagulation and thrombolysis
options.explanation: Anticoagulation and thrombolysis are used for vascular occlusion, not botulism.

options.text: Oral rehydration alone
options.explanation: Oral rehydration alone is insufficient for paralysis and respiratory failure.

question: Which measure is most appropriate to help prevent Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever in at-risk workers?

options.text: Use DEET insect repellent and wear protective clothing
options.explanation: DEET repellent, gloves, and protective clothing help prevent tick exposure and infection.

options.text: Receive a routine human vaccine before exposure
options.explanation: There is no safe, effective human vaccine currently available for routine use.

options.text: Take prophylactic ribavirin after every tick bite
options.explanation: Ribavirin is not established as routine post-exposure prophylaxis for CCHF.

options.text: Avoid all contact with livestock only during winter
options.explanation: CCHF prevention requires year-round tick and blood/body fluid precautions, not just winter avoidance.

question: What is the standard method for diagnosing active lymphatic filariasis?

options.text: Night blood smear showing microfilariae
options.explanation: Active infection is diagnosed by identifying microfilariae in a nocturnal blood smear.

options.text: Stool ova and parasite examination
options.explanation: Stool examination is used for intestinal parasites, not lymphatic filariasis.

options.text: Urine antigen test
options.explanation: Urine antigen testing is not the standard diagnostic method for filarial infection.

options.text: Skin biopsy for larvae
options.explanation: Skin biopsy is not used to detect lymphatic filarial microfilariae in blood.

question: What is La Crosse encephalitis?

options.text: A mosquito-borne viral encephalitis, mainly in children
options.explanation: La Crosse encephalitis is a mosquito-transmitted viral encephalitis that primarily affects children.

options.text: A tick-borne bacterial meningitis in adults
options.explanation: It is not caused by ticks or bacteria, and it is not primarily an adult meningitis.

options.text: A fungal brain infection from contaminated soil
options.explanation: It is not a fungal infection and is not acquired from contaminated soil.

options.text: A parasitic infection causing chronic neuropathy
options.explanation: It is not a parasitic disease and does not typically cause chronic neuropathy.

question: Which statement best describes animal bites?

options.text: They are always life-threatening emergencies.
options.explanation: Most animal bites are not immediately life-threatening.

options.text: They rarely cause serious problems unless infected.
options.explanation: Animal bites are usually not life-threatening, but infection can lead to serious medical problems.

options.text: They only occur from wild animals.
options.explanation: Pet bites are more common than wild animal bites.

options.text: They cannot be prevented.
options.explanation: Many preventive measures can reduce the risk of animal bites.

question: What is lymphatic filariasis?

options.text: A mosquito-borne parasitic infection of the lymphatic system
options.explanation: Lymphatic filariasis is caused by thread-like worms transmitted by mosquitoes that live in the lymphatic system.

options.text: A viral infection causing acute hepatitis and jaundice
options.explanation: Hepatitis and jaundice are features of liver infections, not lymphatic filariasis.

options.text: A bacterial infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
options.explanation: Skin and soft tissue bacterial infection does not describe a mosquito-borne filarial parasite.

options.text: An autoimmune disorder causing lymph node enlargement
options.explanation: Autoimmune lymph node enlargement is not caused by parasitic worms in the lymphatic system.

question: Which statement best describes viral hepatitis A through E and beyond?

options.text: Inflammation of the liver caused by hepatitis viruses A, B, C, D, and E
options.explanation: Hepatitis A through E are viruses that cause inflammation of the liver.

options.text: A bacterial liver infection causing only chronic hepatitis
options.explanation: Viral hepatitis is not bacterial, and A and E do not typically cause chronic infection.

options.text: A parasitic disease limited to the gallbladder
options.explanation: Viral hepatitis affects the liver, not the gallbladder, and is not parasitic.

options.text: An autoimmune disorder caused by vitamin deficiency
options.explanation: It is caused by viruses, not autoimmunity or vitamin deficiency.

question: Which statement best describes parasitic diseases?

options.text: Diseases caused by organisms living on or in a host for food and shelter
options.explanation: Parasitic diseases are caused by parasites that live in or on a host and use it for nourishment and shelter.

options.text: Diseases caused only by bacteria transmitted through air
options.explanation: Bacteria can cause infections, but parasitic diseases are not limited to airborne bacterial spread.

options.text: Diseases caused only by viruses spread by coughing
options.explanation: Viruses cause viral illnesses, not parasitic diseases, and coughing is not the only transmission route.

options.text: Diseases caused only by genetic mutations inherited at birth
options.explanation: Genetic mutations cause inherited disorders, not infections from parasites.

question: How is rabies diagnosed in animals?

options.text: Direct fluorescent antibody test on brain tissue
options.explanation: The DFA test detects rabies virus antigens in animal brain tissue and is the standard diagnostic method.

options.text: Serum antibody titers alone
options.explanation: Serum antibody titers do not reliably diagnose rabies in animals.

options.text: Blood culture for viral growth
options.explanation: Rabies is not diagnosed by blood culture because the virus is not detected this way.

options.text: Urine antigen testing
options.explanation: Urine antigen testing is not used to diagnose rabies.

question: Which measure best helps prevent cysticercosis?

options.text: Wash hands after toileting and before handling food
options.explanation: Handwashing after toileting and before food handling helps prevent fecal-oral transmission of parasite eggs.

options.text: Take antibiotics after eating raw vegetables
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent cysticercosis, which is acquired from ingesting parasite eggs.

options.text: Avoid all dairy products while traveling
options.explanation: Dairy avoidance does not prevent this parasitic infection.

options.text: Use insect repellent daily
options.explanation: Insect repellent does not prevent cysticercosis because it is not spread by insect bites.

question: Who is at risk for paragonimiasis (Paragonimus infection)?

options.text: People eating raw or undercooked crabs or crayfish
options.explanation: Paragonimiasis is acquired by eating raw or undercooked crustaceans such as crabs or crayfish.

options.text: People drinking untreated freshwater only
options.explanation: Untreated freshwater is associated with other parasites, not Paragonimus infection.

options.text: People exposed to mosquito bites in tropical regions
options.explanation: Mosquito bites transmit malaria and other infections, not paragonimiasis.

options.text: People eating undercooked pork or beef
options.explanation: Undercooked pork or beef causes other helminth infections, not Paragonimus.

question: Which measure best helps prevent hemolytic uremic syndrome in children caused by E. coli O157:H7?

options.text: Avoid unpasteurized milk, juice, and undercooked meat
options.explanation: Avoiding contaminated foods and proper food handling reduces exposure to E. coli O157:H7, a common cause of hemolytic uremic syndrome.

options.text: Give prophylactic antibiotics after every diarrheal illness
options.explanation: Routine antibiotics are not recommended for diarrheal illnesses and may worsen some E. coli infections.

options.text: Increase dairy intake to strengthen gut immunity
options.explanation: Dairy intake does not prevent E. coli infection or hemolytic uremic syndrome.

options.text: Use antidiarrheal medications to shorten symptoms
options.explanation: Antidiarrheal drugs can be harmful in infectious diarrhea and do not prevent hemolytic uremic syndrome.

question: What is cryptosporidiosis?

options.text: A parasitic infection causing watery diarrhea
options.explanation: Cryptosporidiosis is caused by a parasite and commonly presents with watery diarrhea.

options.text: A viral infection causing bloody diarrhea
options.explanation: Cryptosporidiosis is not caused by a virus and typically does not cause bloody diarrhea.

options.text: A bacterial infection causing pneumonia
options.explanation: It is not a bacterial respiratory infection and does not primarily cause pneumonia.

options.text: A fungal infection causing skin rash
options.explanation: It is not a fungal skin infection and does not primarily present with rash.

question: What is the recommended treatment for body lice infestation?

options.text: Improve hygiene and launder clothes, bedding, and towels in hot water and hot dryer
options.explanation: Body lice are treated by regular bathing, changing into clean clothes, and hot laundering of contaminated items.

options.text: Start oral antibiotics and isolate the patient for two weeks
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not eradicate lice, and isolation is not the standard treatment.

options.text: Apply topical antifungal cream to affected skin daily
options.explanation: Body lice are parasites, not fungal infections, so antifungal cream is ineffective.

options.text: Use only steroid lotion to reduce itching and inflammation
options.explanation: Steroids may reduce itching but do not kill lice or eliminate infestation.

question: What are foodborne illnesses?

options.text: Infections or irritations of the gastrointestinal tract from contaminated food or beverages
options.explanation: Foodborne illnesses are GI infections or irritations caused by harmful organisms or chemicals in food or drinks.

options.text: Autoimmune diseases affecting the pancreas after eating dairy products
options.explanation: Autoimmune pancreatic disease is not the definition of foodborne illness.

options.text: Chronic inflammatory disorders caused by vitamin deficiencies
options.explanation: Vitamin deficiencies do not cause acute GI illness from contaminated food.

options.text: Respiratory infections spread by airborne food particles
options.explanation: Foodborne illnesses affect the gastrointestinal tract, not the respiratory system.

question: Which statement best describes biodefense in the context of bioterrorism?

options.text: Deliberate release of germs to cause illness or death
options.explanation: This describes bioterrorism itself, not biodefense.

options.text: Medical measures, including vaccines and medicines, to protect against bioterrorism
options.explanation: Biodefense refers to medical measures, including vaccines and medicines, used to protect people against bioterrorism.

options.text: Routine sanitation measures to prevent all infectious diseases
options.explanation: Routine sanitation is general infection control, not the specific preparedness and medical protection of biodefense.

options.text: Use of antibiotics only after a confirmed outbreak
options.explanation: Biodefense includes prevention and preparedness, not only treatment after an outbreak.

question: Which statement best describes viral infections?

options.text: Infections caused by bacteria that respond to antibiotics
options.explanation: Bacterial infections are caused by bacteria and are often treated with antibiotics.

options.text: Infections caused by viruses that invade and use host cells
options.explanation: Viruses invade living cells and use them to replicate, causing viral infections.

options.text: Infections caused only by fungi in the bloodstream
options.explanation: Fungal infections are caused by fungi, not viruses, and are not limited to the bloodstream.

options.text: Infections caused by parasites that live outside cells
options.explanation: Parasitic infections are caused by parasites, which are different from viruses and often live outside cells.

question: Which statement best describes rotavirus infection?

options.text: A viral gastroenteritis causing diarrhea, vomiting, fever, and dehydration
options.explanation: Rotavirus causes gastroenteritis with severe diarrhea, vomiting, fever, and dehydration.

options.text: A bacterial enteritis causing bloody diarrhea and abdominal pain
options.explanation: Bacterial enteritis is not rotavirus and typically causes different features such as bloody diarrhea.

options.text: A parasitic infection causing chronic weight loss and steatorrhea
options.explanation: Parasitic infections usually cause chronic malabsorption, not acute winter gastroenteritis.

options.text: A respiratory virus causing cough, wheezing, and pneumonia
options.explanation: Rotavirus primarily affects the gastrointestinal tract, not the respiratory system.

question: Which statement best describes mycobacterial infections?

options.text: They are caused only by viruses and never affect the lungs.
options.explanation: Mycobacterial infections are bacterial, not viral, and commonly involve the lungs.

options.text: They include tuberculosis, leprosy, and atypical infections, often in immunocompromised patients.
options.explanation: Mycobacteria include organisms causing tuberculosis, leprosy, and atypical infections, which often affect immunocompromised people.

options.text: They always cause severe diarrhea and skin rash.
options.explanation: Diarrhea and rash are not the typical hallmark features of mycobacterial infections.

options.text: They are treated with a single antibiotic in all cases.
options.explanation: Treatment often requires more than one medication, not a single antibiotic in all cases.

question: What are the typical symptoms of human T-cell leukemia virus type 2 (HTLV-2) infection?

options.text: Usually no symptoms; sometimes neuropathy, gait issues, bladder dysfunction, or mild cognitive changes
options.explanation: HTLV-2 is usually asymptomatic, though it may be associated with neurologic and other nonspecific problems.

options.text: High fever, rash, and severe lymphadenopathy in the acute phase
options.explanation: HTLV-2 does not typically cause an acute febrile exanthem with marked lymphadenopathy.

options.text: Painful oral ulcers with recurrent opportunistic infections
options.explanation: Painful oral ulcers and opportunistic infections are not characteristic features of HTLV-2.

options.text: Progressive jaundice and abdominal ascites from liver failure
options.explanation: Liver failure with jaundice and ascites is not a typical presentation of HTLV-2 infection.

question: What is cat scratch disease?

options.text: A Bartonella henselae infection transmitted by cat scratches or bites
options.explanation: Cat scratch disease is caused by Bartonella henselae and is typically transmitted through scratches or bites from infected cats.

options.text: A viral illness spread by cat saliva causing pneumonia
options.explanation: Cat scratch disease is bacterial, not viral, and does not primarily cause pneumonia.

options.text: A fungal skin infection acquired from litter boxes
options.explanation: It is not a fungal infection and is not acquired from litter boxes.

options.text: A parasitic disease transmitted by flea bites only
options.explanation: It is not a parasitic disease and is not transmitted only by flea bites.

question: What is yersiniosis caused by in humans, especially in the United States?

options.text: A viral gastroenteritis from norovirus
options.explanation: Norovirus causes viral gastroenteritis, not yersiniosis.

options.text: An infection by Yersinia enterocolitica
options.explanation: Yersiniosis is an infectious disease caused most commonly by Yersinia enterocolitica.

options.text: A fungal infection from Candida albicans
options.explanation: Candida albicans causes candidiasis, not a Yersinia infection.

options.text: A parasitic infection from Giardia lamblia
options.explanation: Giardia lamblia causes giardiasis, not yersiniosis.

question: Which public health activity is performed by the CDC to help prevent or control yersiniosis?

options.text: Monitors Y. enterocolitica through FoodNet and investigates outbreaks
options.explanation: CDC tracks Y. enterocolitica infections through FoodNet and investigates outbreaks to control spread and improve prevention.

options.text: Inspects imported foods and milk pasteurization plants
options.explanation: This is the role of the FDA, not the CDC.

options.text: Regulates drinking water safety and supplies
options.explanation: This is the role of the EPA, not the CDC.

options.text: Monitors food animals and meat processing quality
options.explanation: This is the role of the USDA, not the CDC.

question: What is tetanus?

options.text: A Clostridium infection causing painful muscle spasms and jaw locking
options.explanation: Tetanus is caused by Clostridium bacteria and produces painful muscle tightening and lockjaw.

options.text: A viral illness causing rash and joint pain
options.explanation: Tetanus is not caused by a virus and does not typically present with rash and joint pain.

options.text: A fungal infection causing skin scaling and itching
options.explanation: Tetanus is not a fungal skin infection and does not cause scaling or itching.

options.text: A parasitic disease causing chronic diarrhea
options.explanation: Tetanus is not a parasitic intestinal illness and does not primarily cause chronic diarrhea.

question: Which patients should receive antiparasitic treatment for American trypanosomiasis (Chagas disease)?

options.text: All acute, congenital, immunosuppressed, and all children with chronic infection
options.explanation: Treatment is recommended for acute, congenital, immunosuppressed, and all children with chronic Chagas infection.

options.text: Only patients with advanced cardiac disease
options.explanation: Advanced cardiac disease may need symptomatic care, but treatment is not limited to those patients.

options.text: Only symptomatic adults with chronic infection
options.explanation: Adults with chronic infection may benefit, but treatment is not restricted only to symptomatic adults.

options.text: Only travelers returning from endemic areas
options.explanation: Travel history alone does not determine treatment; confirmed infection and clinical category do.

question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for Stenotrophomonas maltophilia infection?

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
options.explanation: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is the recommended first-line therapy for S. maltophilia.

options.text: Vancomycin
options.explanation: Vancomycin does not reliably treat this gram-negative organism.

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate
options.explanation: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is usually ineffective against S. maltophilia.

options.text: Metronidazole
options.explanation: Metronidazole has no activity against S. maltophilia.

question: Which statement best describes treatment of Acanthamoeba keratitis and granulomatous amebic encephalitis (GAE)?

options.text: Keratitis may respond to prescription eye drops; GAE is usually fatal.
options.explanation: Acanthamoeba keratitis can be treated with prescription eye medications, while GAE has a very poor prognosis and is often fatal.

options.text: Both conditions are easily cured with oral antibiotics alone.
options.explanation: Acanthamoeba is not treated with oral antibiotics alone, and GAE is not easily cured.

options.text: Only surgery is effective for both eye and CNS infection.
options.explanation: Surgery is not the standard effective treatment for either keratitis or CNS infection.

options.text: No treatment exists for either condition.
options.explanation: Treatment options exist for keratitis and some non-CNS infections, so it is not true that no treatment exists.

question: Which laboratory approach is used to diagnose leishmaniasis?

options.text: Microscopy and culture of tissue specimens
options.explanation: Leishmaniasis is diagnosed by examining tissue specimens such as skin or bone marrow for parasites using microscopy and culture.

options.text: Stool ova and parasite examination
options.explanation: Stool ova and parasite testing is used for intestinal parasites, not leishmaniasis.

options.text: Urine antigen testing
options.explanation: Urine antigen testing is not a standard diagnostic method for leishmaniasis.

options.text: Sputum acid-fast staining
options.explanation: Acid-fast staining is used for mycobacterial infections, not Leishmania.

question: What best describes norovirus infection?

options.text: A viral gastroenteritis causing diarrhea, vomiting, and stomach pain
options.explanation: Norovirus causes gastroenteritis with diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain.

options.text: A bacterial pneumonia causing cough and fever
options.explanation: Norovirus does not cause pneumonia or primarily respiratory symptoms.

options.text: A parasitic liver infection causing jaundice
options.explanation: Norovirus is not a parasite and does not primarily infect the liver.

options.text: A fungal skin infection causing itching and rash
options.explanation: Norovirus is not a fungal infection and does not primarily cause skin disease.

question: What is Hansen's disease?

options.text: A rare bacterial infection affecting skin, nerves, and mucous membranes
options.explanation: Hansen's disease, or leprosy, is caused by Mycobacterium leprae and primarily affects the skin, nerves, and mucous membranes.

options.text: A viral infection causing acute respiratory illness
options.explanation: It is not a viral respiratory infection; it is a chronic bacterial disease.

options.text: An autoimmune disorder of the joints
options.explanation: It is not an autoimmune joint disorder; it is an infectious disease caused by bacteria.

options.text: A fungal infection limited to the lungs
options.explanation: It is not a fungal lung infection; Hansen's disease involves skin and peripheral nerves.

question: What is the best protection against sexually transmitted diseases if a teen chooses to have sex?

options.text: Abstinence
options.explanation: Abstinence is the only completely safe way, but it is not protection during sex.

options.text: Latex condoms
options.explanation: Latex condoms provide the best protection against STDs when having sex.

options.text: Oral contraceptive pills
options.explanation: Oral contraceptive pills help prevent pregnancy but do not protect against STDs.

options.text: Withdrawal method
options.explanation: Withdrawal is unreliable for pregnancy prevention and does not protect against STDs.

question: How is Chapare hemorrhagic fever (CHHF) diagnosed?

options.text: By RT-PCR on clinical specimens and serologic assays such as ELISA or indirect immunofluorescence
options.explanation: CHHF is diagnosed using specific molecular assays like RT-PCR and serologic tests such as ELISA or indirect immunofluorescence.

options.text: By stool culture and rapid antigen testing only
options.explanation: Stool culture and rapid antigen testing are not established diagnostic methods for CHHF.

options.text: By chest radiography and serum amylase levels
options.explanation: Chest radiography and serum amylase do not identify Chapare virus infection.

options.text: By skin biopsy showing granulomas
options.explanation: Skin biopsy with granulomas is not a diagnostic feature of CHHF.

question: Who is at increased risk for hepatitis C infection?

options.text: People with multiple sex partners or prior STDs
options.explanation: Multiple sex partners and a history of sexually transmitted disease increase hepatitis C risk.

options.text: People with seasonal allergies
options.explanation: Seasonal allergies do not increase exposure to bloodborne viruses like hepatitis C.

options.text: People who exercise regularly
options.explanation: Regular exercise does not affect hepatitis C transmission risk.

options.text: People with lactose intolerance
options.explanation: Lactose intolerance is unrelated to hepatitis C infection risk.

question: Who is at increased risk for Alkhurma hemorrhagic fever (AHF)?

options.text: People with tick exposure or contact with livestock
options.explanation: AHF risk is linked to tick bites and contact with domestic animals or livestock.

options.text: People who drink unpasteurized milk
options.explanation: Unpasteurized milk transmission has not been described for AHF.

options.text: People with close contact with infected patients
options.explanation: No human-to-human transmission of AHF has been documented.

options.text: People with mosquito exposure only
options.explanation: Mosquitoes are not known vectors for Alkhurma hemorrhagic fever.

question: Which statement best describes Campylobacter infection?

options.text: A foodborne illness from raw or undercooked poultry causing diarrhea and cramps
options.explanation: Campylobacter is a common foodborne illness linked to poultry and typically causes diarrhea, cramping, abdominal pain, fever, and nausea.

options.text: A mosquito-borne viral illness causing jaundice and rash
options.explanation: Campylobacter is not spread by mosquitoes and does not typically cause jaundice and rash.

options.text: A sexually transmitted infection causing urethral discharge
options.explanation: Campylobacter is not a sexually transmitted infection and does not primarily cause urethral discharge.

options.text: A waterborne parasitic infection causing chronic constipation
options.explanation: Campylobacter is not a parasitic infection and usually causes acute diarrhea rather than chronic constipation.

question: What is athlete's foot?

options.text: A fungal infection of the skin, usually between the toes
options.explanation: Athlete's foot is a common fungal infection that often affects the spaces between the toes.

options.text: A bacterial infection of the toenail bed
options.explanation: Bacterial infections of the toenail bed are different conditions and do not describe athlete's foot.

options.text: A viral rash caused by sweating
options.explanation: Athlete's foot is caused by fungus, not a virus or sweating alone.

options.text: An autoimmune disorder of the feet
options.explanation: Athlete's foot is an infection, not an autoimmune disease.

question: Which measure best helps prevent ascariasis infection?

options.text: Wash hands and avoid ingesting soil contaminated with human feces
options.explanation: Ascariasis is prevented by handwashing and avoiding fecally contaminated soil or food.

options.text: Take prophylactic antibiotics after eating raw vegetables
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent helminth infection from contaminated soil or produce.

options.text: Use insect repellent before handling food
options.explanation: Insect repellent does not prevent transmission of Ascaris eggs.

options.text: Boil drinking water only after symptoms appear
options.explanation: Boiling water after symptoms appear does not prevent initial fecal-oral exposure.

question: How can fascioliasis (Fasciola infection) be prevented?

options.text: Avoid eating raw watercress and other aquatic plants from endemic areas
options.explanation: Avoiding raw watercress and other aquatic plants reduces ingestion of Fasciola metacercariae.

options.text: Receive a routine vaccine before travel
options.explanation: No vaccine is available to protect people against Fasciola infection.

options.text: Use insect repellent to prevent snail bites
options.explanation: Fasciola is acquired by ingestion, not by insect or snail bites.

options.text: Take daily antimalarial prophylaxis
options.explanation: Antimalarial prophylaxis does not prevent fascioliasis.

question: Who is at risk for Kyasanur Forest Disease (KFD)?

options.text: People with rural outdoor exposure in Karnataka, especially hunters and forest workers
options.explanation: KFD risk is highest in people with recreational or occupational exposure to rural or forested areas in Karnataka where infected ticks are present.

options.text: Urban residents with no animal or tick exposure
options.explanation: Urban residents without tick exposure are not the typical at-risk group for KFD.

options.text: Travelers only in coastal Tamil Nadu cities
options.explanation: KFD is linked to rural Karnataka tick exposure, not specifically to coastal Tamil Nadu cities.

options.text: Healthcare workers caring for hospitalized patients
options.explanation: KFD is not primarily spread person-to-person in hospitals, so healthcare workers are not the main risk group.

question: Which description best defines mumps?

options.text: A viral illness causing fever, headache, and swollen salivary glands
options.explanation: Mumps is a viral illness that typically causes fever, headache, and swelling of the salivary glands.

options.text: A bacterial infection causing painful lymph node enlargement
options.explanation: Mumps is not caused by bacteria and does not primarily present as lymph node infection.

options.text: An autoimmune disease causing parotid gland destruction
options.explanation: Mumps is infectious, not an autoimmune disorder, and does not destroy the parotid gland.

options.text: A fungal infection causing facial swelling and rash
options.explanation: Mumps is not a fungal infection and does not characteristically cause rash.

question: Which statement about human lice is correct?

options.text: They can hop or fly between people.
options.explanation: Lice crawl and cannot hop or fly.

options.text: Dogs and cats commonly spread human lice.
options.explanation: Pets do not spread human lice.

options.text: They spread mainly by close person-to-person contact.
options.explanation: Human lice are most commonly spread by close person-to-person contact.

options.text: They feed on skin flakes rather than blood.
options.explanation: Lice survive by feeding on human blood, not skin flakes.

question: Which description best defines crusted scabies?

options.text: A severe, highly contagious scabies with thick crusts and many mites
options.explanation: Crusted scabies is a severe form of scabies with thick crusts containing numerous mites and eggs.

options.text: A mild scabies rash with intense itching only
options.explanation: Typical itching and rash may be absent in crusted scabies, so this describes ordinary scabies poorly.

options.text: A fungal infection causing scaly plaques on the scalp
options.explanation: Crusted scabies is caused by mites, not fungi.

options.text: A bacterial skin infection with honey-colored crusts
options.explanation: Honey-colored crusts suggest impetigo, not scabies.

question: Which presentation is most consistent with Omsk hemorrhagic fever after a 3–8 day incubation period?

options.text: Sudden fever, chills, headache, myalgia, then bleeding and cytopenias
options.explanation: OHF typically begins abruptly with fever, chills, headache, severe myalgia, followed by bleeding and low blood counts.

options.text: Gradual cough, wheezing, and pleuritic chest pain
options.explanation: Respiratory symptoms like cough and wheezing are not the classic early features of OHF.

options.text: Painless jaundice with dark urine and pruritus
options.explanation: Jaundice and pruritus suggest hepatobiliary disease, not hemorrhagic fever.

options.text: Chronic diarrhea with weight loss and oral thrush
options.explanation: Chronic diarrhea and thrush suggest immunodeficiency or gastrointestinal disease, not OHF.

question: What is infectious mononucleosis most commonly caused by?

options.text: Epstein-Barr virus spread through saliva
options.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis is usually caused by Epstein-Barr virus and spreads through saliva.

options.text: Influenza virus spread by droplets
options.explanation: Influenza causes respiratory illness, not classic mononucleosis.

options.text: Streptococcus pyogenes infection
options.explanation: Streptococcal pharyngitis can mimic sore throat but does not cause mono.

options.text: Cytomegalovirus from contaminated food
options.explanation: CMV can cause a mono-like illness, but EBV is the usual cause.

question: Which set of findings is most consistent with tularemia?

options.text: Fever, chills, headache, and swollen painful lymph nodes
options.explanation: Tularemia commonly causes fever, chills, headache, weakness, and painful lymphadenopathy.

options.text: Jaundice, pruritus, and pale stools
options.explanation: Jaundice, pruritus, and pale stools suggest cholestatic liver disease, not tularemia.

options.text: Polyuria, polydipsia, and weight gain
options.explanation: Polyuria, polydipsia, and weight gain are more consistent with endocrine disorders such as diabetes or hypothyroidism.

options.text: Bradycardia, constipation, and cold intolerance
options.explanation: Bradycardia, constipation, and cold intolerance are classic for hypothyroidism, not tularemia.

question: Who is at risk for Chapare hemorrhagic fever (CHHF)?

options.text: People exposed to infected rodents or their excreta
options.explanation: CHHF is a rodent-borne arenavirus infection acquired by direct contact or inhalation of aerosolized rodent urine or feces.

options.text: People bitten by mosquitoes carrying the virus
options.explanation: Mosquitoes are not known vectors for Chapare virus transmission.

options.text: People exposed only to contaminated water
options.explanation: Contaminated water is not the typical source of Chapare virus infection.

options.text: People with routine contact with healthy livestock
options.explanation: Healthy livestock are not recognized reservoirs or sources of CHHF.

question: Which diagnostic approach is most appropriate for Acanthamoeba keratitis?

options.text: Eye specialist evaluation with corneal scraping culture and confocal microscopy
options.explanation: Acanthamoeba keratitis is diagnosed by ophthalmic examination, corneal scraping, and confocal microscopy.

options.text: Brain MRI with lumbar puncture and CSF culture
options.explanation: Brain MRI and lumbar puncture are used for granulomatous amebic encephalitis, not isolated keratitis.

options.text: Blood culture and serum antibody testing
options.explanation: Blood cultures and serum antibodies are not the standard diagnostic methods for Acanthamoeba keratitis.

options.text: Skin biopsy of a disseminated lesion
options.explanation: Skin biopsy is useful for disseminated Acanthamoeba infection, not primary corneal disease.

question: Which presentation is most typical of Stenotrophomonas maltophilia infection when symptoms occur?

options.text: Pneumonia and bacteremia
options.explanation: Stenotrophomonas maltophilia most commonly causes pneumonia and bacteremia when it produces symptomatic infection.

options.text: Vesicular rash and oral ulcers
options.explanation: Vesicular rash and oral ulcers are not typical manifestations of this bacterial infection.

options.text: Severe diarrhea and jaundice
options.explanation: Severe diarrhea and jaundice suggest gastrointestinal or hepatobiliary disease, not this infection.

options.text: Migratory arthritis and urethritis
options.explanation: Migratory arthritis and urethritis are more consistent with reactive or sexually transmitted conditions.

question: Which statement best describes human T-cell leukemia virus type 1 (HTLV-1)?

options.text: A retrovirus infecting T cells that may cause ATL and HAM/TSP
options.explanation: HTLV-1 is a retrovirus that infects T cells and can later cause adult T-cell leukemia and HAM/TSP.

options.text: A DNA virus causing acute hepatitis and cirrhosis
options.explanation: HTLV-1 is not a DNA virus and does not primarily cause hepatitis or cirrhosis.

options.text: A bacterium causing chronic lymphadenitis and fever
options.explanation: HTLV-1 is a virus, not a bacterium, and it does not cause chronic lymphadenitis.

options.text: A prion causing progressive dementia and ataxia
options.explanation: HTLV-1 is not a prion and does not cause prion diseases like dementia and ataxia.

question: Which treatment regimen is used for neurosyphilis?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin for 3 days
options.explanation: Amoxicillin is not the standard treatment for neurosyphilis and the duration is too short.

options.text: IV penicillin or daily IM penicillin with probenecid for 10–14 days
options.explanation: Neurosyphilis is treated with penicillin given intravenously or by daily intramuscular injections plus probenecid for 10–14 days.

options.text: Single-dose azithromycin
options.explanation: Azithromycin is not an accepted therapy for neurosyphilis.

options.text: Topical benzathine penicillin only
options.explanation: Topical benzathine penicillin cannot treat central nervous system infection.

question: Which measure is recommended to prevent Alkhurma hemorrhagic fever (AHF)?

options.text: Avoid tick-infested areas and use tick repellents
options.explanation: Prevention relies on avoiding tick exposure and using repellents because no specific treatment or prophylaxis exists.

options.text: Take a specific antiviral prophylaxis
options.explanation: No specific antiviral prophylaxis is currently available for AHF prevention.

options.text: Receive a routine vaccine before travel
options.explanation: There is no routine vaccine available for Alkhurma hemorrhagic fever.

options.text: Use antibiotics after tick bites
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent this viral infection after tick bites.

question: Which research focus is being pursued for shingles (varicella-zoster virus) by NINDS-supported studies?

options.text: Understanding viral latency, reactivation, and persistent pain mechanisms
options.explanation: NINDS research on shingles focuses on VZV latency, reactivation, neuronal transport, and persistent pain.

options.text: Developing antibiotics to eradicate bacterial skin infections
options.explanation: Shingles is caused by a virus, not a bacterial infection, so antibiotics are not the main research focus.

options.text: Studying autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
options.explanation: Autoimmune beta-cell destruction relates to diabetes, not varicella-zoster virus research.

options.text: Testing antifungal drugs for recurrent candidiasis
options.explanation: Candidiasis is a fungal infection and is unrelated to shingles research.

question: Which statement best describes eye infections?

options.text: They are infections caused only by bacteria in the cornea.
options.explanation: Eye infections are not caused only by bacteria and can involve more than the cornea.

options.text: They can be caused by bacteria, fungi, or viruses in different eye parts.
options.explanation: Eye infections may be caused by bacteria, fungi, or viruses and can affect different parts of one or both eyes.

options.text: They always affect both eyes and never cause discharge.
options.explanation: Eye infections may affect one or both eyes and can cause discharge.

options.text: They are limited to eyelid skin and never affect vision.
options.explanation: Eye infections are not limited to the eyelid and can cause vision problems.

question: Which presentation is most consistent with Lemierre syndrome in children and adolescents?

options.text: Severe sore throat, persistent fever, neck pain, and tender jugular swelling
options.explanation: Lemierre syndrome typically starts with pharyngitis and fever, then progresses to neck pain and tender internal jugular vein swelling.

options.text: Painless jaundice with pruritus and pale stools
options.explanation: This pattern suggests cholestatic liver disease, not Lemierre syndrome.

options.text: Sudden unilateral facial droop with slurred speech
options.explanation: This describes a neurologic event such as stroke, not a throat infection complication.

options.text: Intermittent wheezing with exercise-induced dyspnea
options.explanation: This is more consistent with asthma or reactive airway disease, not Lemierre syndrome.

question: Which test is used for specific diagnosis of Lujo hemorrhagic fever during the acute phase?

options.text: Blood culture on routine media
options.explanation: Routine blood culture does not detect Lujo virus, an RNA hemorrhagic fever virus.

options.text: RT-PCR for Lujo virus RNA
options.explanation: Specific molecular detection by RT-PCR is used to diagnose Lujo virus infection in clinical specimens.

options.text: Widal test
options.explanation: Widal test is used for typhoid fever, not viral hemorrhagic fevers.

options.text: Heterophile antibody test
options.explanation: Heterophile antibody testing is used for infectious mononucleosis, not Lujo hemorrhagic fever.

question: Which laboratory test combination is used to diagnose Hendra virus disease during acute and convalescent phases?

options.text: Serum IgG/IgM ELISA, RT-PCR, and virus isolation
options.explanation: Hendra virus diagnosis relies on antibody detection, RT-PCR for viral RNA, and virus isolation from appropriate specimens.

options.text: Blood culture and Gram stain
options.explanation: Blood culture and Gram stain do not detect Hendra virus, which is a viral infection.

options.text: Widal test and stool microscopy
options.explanation: Widal test and stool microscopy are used for enteric infections, not Hendra virus disease.

options.text: Skin biopsy with immunofluorescence
options.explanation: Skin biopsy with immunofluorescence is not the standard diagnostic approach for Hendra virus.

question: Which statement best describes Klebsiella infections?

options.text: Community-acquired viral illnesses in healthy children
options.explanation: Klebsiella infections are bacterial and usually healthcare-associated, not viral community illnesses.

options.text: Healthcare-associated bacterial infections causing pneumonia, bloodstream, wound, or meningitis
options.explanation: Klebsiella commonly causes healthcare-associated bacterial infections including pneumonia, bloodstream, wound, and meningitis.

options.text: Autoimmune disorders affecting the lungs and kidneys
options.explanation: Klebsiella is an infectious bacterium, not an autoimmune disease.

options.text: Parasitic infections spread mainly by contaminated food
options.explanation: Klebsiella is not a parasite and is not mainly spread through contaminated food.

question: Which advice best helps prevent infections during pregnancy and protect the baby?

options.text: Eat raw meat to increase iron intake
options.explanation: Raw or undercooked meat can carry infections such as toxoplasmosis and should be avoided.

options.text: Share drinks to build immunity
options.explanation: Sharing food or drinks can spread infections and is not recommended.

options.text: Avoid cat litter and wash hands frequently
options.explanation: Avoiding cat litter and frequent handwashing reduce exposure to infections that can harm the fetus.

options.text: Take any vaccine without checking safety
options.explanation: Not all medicines and vaccines are safe in pregnancy, so they must be checked first.

question: Which statement best describes babesiosis?

options.text: A tick-borne infection of red blood cells caused mainly by Babesia microti
options.explanation: Babesiosis is caused by Babesia parasites that infect red blood cells and are commonly transmitted by Ixodes ticks.

options.text: A viral illness transmitted by mosquitoes causing liver inflammation
options.explanation: Babesiosis is not a mosquito-borne viral hepatitis syndrome.

options.text: A bacterial infection of white blood cells spread by fleas
options.explanation: Babesiosis is not a flea-borne bacterial infection of white blood cells.

options.text: A fungal disease acquired from contaminated food
options.explanation: Babesiosis is not a foodborne fungal infection.

question: What is the usual diagnostic test for neurocysticercosis?

options.text: Stool ova and parasite examination
options.explanation: Stool testing may detect intestinal tapeworm infection, not brain cysticercosis.

options.text: MRI or CT brain scans
options.explanation: Neurocysticercosis is usually diagnosed with MRI or CT brain imaging.

options.text: Chest X-ray
options.explanation: Chest X-ray does not diagnose cysticercosis in the brain.

options.text: Skin biopsy of lesions
options.explanation: Skin biopsy is not the standard test for diagnosing neurocysticercosis.

question: Which approach is most appropriate for diagnosing suspected foodborne illness?

options.text: History, physical exam, and stool culture when needed
options.explanation: Diagnosis relies on symptom and exposure history, exam, and stool testing when indicated.

options.text: Immediate abdominal CT scan for all patients
options.explanation: CT scanning is not the routine first-line test for foodborne illness.

options.text: Serum troponin and ECG testing
options.explanation: Troponin and ECG evaluate cardiac disease, not gastrointestinal infection.

options.text: Skin prick allergy testing only
options.explanation: Allergy testing does not diagnose infectious foodborne illness.

question: Which action is most effective for helping stop the spread of germs?

options.text: Wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds
options.explanation: Hand washing with soap and water for at least 20 seconds is one of the most effective ways to reduce germ spread.

options.text: Touch your face less often
options.explanation: Avoiding face touching helps, but it is not as effective as proper hand washing.

options.text: Use hand sanitizer only after meals
options.explanation: Hand sanitizer can help, but it should be used more broadly than only after meals.

options.text: Cough into your hands
options.explanation: Coughing into your hands spreads germs to surfaces and other people.

question: Which description best defines body lice infestation?

options.text: Parasites living on clothing and bedding, feeding on blood
options.explanation: Body lice live on clothing and bedding and move to skin only to feed on blood.

options.text: Ticks that burrow into skin and lay eggs in hair follicles
options.explanation: Ticks do not live on clothing seams or primarily infest people through bedding.

options.text: Mosquitoes that breed in standing water and bite at night
options.explanation: Mosquitoes are flying insects that do not live on clothing or bedding.

options.text: Fleas that live permanently on household pets and carpets
options.explanation: Fleas mainly infest animals and carpets, not clothing seams on humans.

question: Which laboratory finding and test best support the diagnosis of lymphocytic choriomeningitis (LCM)?

options.text: Leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and CSF IgM/IgG antibodies
options.explanation: LCM commonly causes leukopenia and thrombocytopenia, and diagnosis is usually confirmed by IgM and IgG antibodies in CSF and serum.

options.text: Eosinophilia with elevated serum amylase
options.explanation: Eosinophilia and elevated amylase are not typical findings in LCM.

options.text: Marked neutrophilia with positive blood culture
options.explanation: Neutrophilia with positive blood culture suggests bacterial infection, not LCM.

options.text: Hypercalcemia with elevated alkaline phosphatase
options.explanation: Hypercalcemia and elevated alkaline phosphatase are unrelated to LCM diagnosis.

question: Which measure is the most effective way to prevent the spread of infections in hospitals?

options.text: Proper hand washing
options.explanation: Proper hand washing is the most effective infection control measure in hospitals.
